title: Wet-felting a soap covering
subtitle: A merino-wrapped bar of soap, made in a single hour at the kitchen sink
excerpt: Wrap a bar of soap in cross-hatched layers of merino roving, wet down with hot soapy water, rub the fibres against the soap surface. The wool felts into a tight skin around the bar; the bar lathers through the wool when used. A one-hour project that teaches the whole wet-felting motion at a scale that fails forgivingly.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional wet-felting practice rather than a single book citation. The soap-as-resist technique is documented across multiple modern fibre-arts guilds and community workshops; the underlying technique (cross-hatched layers, soap as the felting agent, hot-water-and-friction to bond the fibres) is the same as the kitchen-counter flat-panel method documented in Mongolian and Anatolian felting traditions. The bar-of-soap-as-resist twist is a twentieth-century craft-fair innovation that turns the resist into the finished object.

recipeTools.notes: A clean cotton tea towel for the working surface and for the final pat-dry.
Olive-oil soap (Aleppo, castile) is the traditional wet-felting soap. Any soap with a slow lather works.
Optional but helpful for the first few projects. A piece of nylon tulle or cheap netting laid over the wool stops the surface from disturbing while rubbing.
Optional. A bamboo or reed mat lets the bar be rolled rather than rubbed in the final firming stage; faster and tougher.

glossaryTerms.definition: Bonding loose wool fibres into a dense fabric using hot soapy water and friction. The barbed scales on each wool fibre interlock under agitation; soap reduces the surface tension so water and heat reach the fibre cores. Distinct from needle felting, which uses barbed needles to interlock fibres without water.

Wool fibre drawn into a long ribbon with the fibres lying roughly parallel along its length. The form most felting and spinning sells in. Merino roving is the easiest to felt for beginners — fine fibre, smooth scales, fast bond.

Layers of roving laid at right angles to each other — the first layer one direction, the second across it, and so on. The cross-hatch is what makes the felt strong in both directions; layers laid in parallel only give strength along one axis and tear easily across the other.

The shrinking stage of wet felting. After the fibres have bonded into a loose pre-felt, fulling — more vigorous rubbing or rolling, sometimes with hotter water — shrinks the felt down to its final tight size. A felted soap covering should shrink by about 20-30% from its pre-felt size during fulling.

content.text: A wet-felted soap covering is the smallest, fastest, most forgiving introduction to
wet felting

. The bar of soap inside the wool is both the resist (the thing the felt forms around) and the lather source (the soap that wets the wool and makes it slide). The finished object is a useful washing thing: the wool exfoliates skin while the soap bar inside lathers through. As the soap wears down, the wool jacket shrinks to fit the smaller bar, and the whole thing lasts about a third longer than an unwrapped bar of the same soap.
The technique here is the kitchen-sink wet-felt: a bowl of hot water, a bar of soap, a small pile of merino
roving

, and your hands. No special equipment. Working time: about an hour, including the fulling. The motion you learn here scales up to flat panels, wet-felted vessels (where the bar of soap is replaced by a plastic ball or a flat resist), and wet-felted hats. The bar-of-soap version fails forgivingly: an under-felted soap covering still works as a soap; the worst case is the wool comes off the bar after a few uses and is discarded.

attrs.title: Before you start
attrs.body:
Hot water. The water for wet felting wants to be hot — about 50-60°C, hotter than comfortable hand-washing temperature. Test with the back of the wrist; if it's uncomfortable but not painful, it's about right. Keep a kettle ready to top up; the bowl cools fast.

Soap on hands. Wet-felting soap is olive-oil-based (castile, Aleppo) and very gentle, but extended wet hands in hot soap will dry skin badly. Hand cream after the session is sensible.

Wool dust. Tearing roving releases some short loose fibres into the air — not as much as carding does, but enough that someone with wool dust sensitivity should work outdoors or in a ventilated room. The wet-felted version is much less dusty than dry needle-felting.

Clothing. Wear a long apron or old clothes; soap suds and wet wool fibres travel further than you'd expect. The kitchen sink is the natural working position because it has water, a flat surface, and tolerates spillage.

content.text: What you need
A bar of soap — 80 to 120 g.

Almost any soap works. Olive-oil soaps (Aleppo, castile) are the traditional choice and lather slowly, which suits the long use. Cold-process homemade soaps work; commercial soaps work. Avoid heavily-scented soaps if you dislike strong-smelling washing things; the scent permeates the wool for the life of the bar.
Merino roving — 15 to 20 g.

Fine merino (19-21 micron, sold as combed top in any wet-felting supplier) is the easiest fibre to felt for beginners. Other breeds (Romney, Bluefaced Leicester, Shetland) felt cleanly but more slowly; corriedale is a good intermediate. Pick one or two colours; cross-hatched colour layers come out as a tweedy blend, not as distinct stripes.
Hot water and a bowl.
About a litre of hot water in a bowl. A small jug or cup for dribbling water onto the wool is helpful.
A cotton tea towel.
Folded under the work to catch drips; doubles as the final pat-dry. A felting net (or piece of nylon tulle) over the wool while you rub helps for the first few projects but isn't essential.
Method
Step 1 — Tear the roving into thin strips.

Hold a length of roving in both hands about 25 cm apart and pull gently. The fibres separate at the staple length into a long thin strip. Don't cut — torn ends blend cleanly; cut ends form a visible seam line in the finished felt. Tear off five or six strips, each about the length of the soap bar plus 10 cm.
Step 2 — Lay the first layer along the bar.

Place the soap bar on the tea towel. Lay two or three roving strips along the long axis of the bar, overlapping the ends so the wool covers the bar completely lengthways with about 3 cm of wool overhanging each end. The wool should sit lightly across the soap, not be pressed down — about half a centimetre of wool depth across the bar.
Step 3 — Lay the second layer across the bar.

Lay two or three more strips across the short axis of the bar (at right angles to the first layer), overlapping along the long axis with 3 cm overhanging each side. The cross-hatch is the bit that matters: without it, the felt has strength in one direction and tears in the other. Lift the bar carefully (the wool is loose at this stage) and check the under-side of the bar — fold the overhanging strips of both layers around to cover the bottom of the bar so the whole bar is wrapped in two cross-hatched layers of wool.
Add a third layer at a 45-degree angle if you want extra strength or thickness,
cross-hatched
against both previous layers. Two layers is the working minimum; three layers gives a thicker, more luxurious feel and slightly longer life.
Step 4 — Wet the wool.

Holding the wrapped bar over the bowl, dribble hot water across the wool until the wool is just wet through. Don't drown it — too much water makes the wool slip rather than felt. The wool should look damp and stuck to the bar, not dripping. Press the wool gently against the bar with both hands to mat the fibres in.
Step 5 — Rub the surface.

Hold the wet bar in one hand and rub the wool surface in straight passes with the flat of the other hand for one to two minutes. The soap dissolves into the wool as you rub — that's the felting agent. Rub each face of the bar in turn, including the edges and ends. The first stage of felting takes about ten minutes of steady rubbing; the wool should start feeling like fabric, not loose fibres. If the wool starts pilling at the surface, slow down — pilling means the surface is moving faster than the layers underneath.
Step 6 — Pinch test.

After ten minutes, pinch the surface of the wool. If you can lift only one or two fibres away from the rest, the felt has formed. If a tuft comes away in your fingers, keep rubbing — the bond is not deep enough yet. The bond should be uniform across the bar; thin spots will tear in use.
Step 7 — Full the felt.
Once the pinch test passes, the felt is loose pre-felt.
Fulling

is the firming-down stage: more aggressive rubbing with hotter water, often rolling the bar back and forth on a bamboo mat or between the palms with firm pressure. Add a splash of hot water and rub vigorously for another five to ten minutes — the felt should shrink visibly by about 20-30% and become noticeably tighter to the bar. The finished felt should be firm to the touch and shouldn't shift across the bar surface.
Step 8 — Rinse and shape.

Rinse the bar under cold water until the water runs clear of soap — squeeze the bar gently to flush the soap out. The cold-water shock helps lock the felt. Pat the bar dry on the tea towel, smooth the wool against the soap to settle any folds, and leave on a soap dish or on a clean towel for 24-48 hours to dry fully before first use.
Care + use

Use the felted soap as you would any bar of soap — wet the wool surface, rub between the hands or directly on skin, lather builds through the wool. Between uses, leave on a draining soap dish so the wool dries (a wet wool surface sitting in standing water stays wet too long and can sour). The wool layer shrinks slowly as the soap bar shrinks, so the cover stays roughly snug throughout the bar's life. When the soap is used up, the wool can be cut open, the fragment of soap retrieved if you like, and the wool itself composted (clean wool composts in about a year).
Common mistakes
The wool slides off the bar instead of felting.

The water was too cool, or the wool was too thick to wet through. Add hotter water and press the wool flat against the bar firmly before rubbing. Merino felts at 50-55°C; cooler water just makes wet wool.
The surface pills as you rub.

Rubbing was too vigorous in the first stage. The first ten minutes is light steady rubbing to bond the fibres; the vigorous fulling is the second stage, after the surface has formed. If pilling shows up early, slow down and use a felting net over the work for the first five minutes.
The felt has thin spots where the soap shows through.

The layers weren't even — the wool ribbons were laid thicker in some places and thinner in others. If the thin spot is small, lay a fresh small piece of roving over the spot, re-wet, and felt it in. If the spot is large, the bar would have to be re-wrapped from scratch.
The finished felt is too loose — it slips on the bar.

Fulling was cut short. Re-wet with hot water and rub for another five to ten minutes. The felt should shrink another 5-10% in extra fulling; if it doesn't shrink, the bond is already at its limit and the cover will be slightly loose but still usable.
Where this technique goes next

The wet-felting motion learned on a soap covering is the same motion that makes flat panels, felted bowls (the bar of soap is replaced by a wedge of bubble wrap as a flat resist), wet-felted hats (a head-shaped resist of plastic or sponge), and wet-felted slippers. The pattern always begins the same way: cross-hatched layers, hot soapy water, ten minutes of careful rubbing, a pinch test, then fulling. The soap covering is the kitchen-sink scale; everything else is the same craft at larger sizes.
